back_translation: When it came to the month of Nisan, in the twentieth year of King Artaxerxes, when wine was placed before His presence, I lifted up the wine and presented it to the king. I had never before had a sad face before His presence.
The king said to me, "Why is your face sad, when you are not sick? This must be sorrow of heart." So I became very afraid.

I said to the king, "May the king have long life everlasting. Why should my face not be sad, when the city which is the burial-place of my ancestors lies in ruins, and its gates have been burned with fire?"
The king said to me, "What is your request?" So I prayed to the God of heaven.

Then I said to the king, "If it pleases the king, and if your servant is favorable in your sight, may you send me to the province of Judah, to the city which is the burial-place of my ancestors, so that I may rebuild that city anew."
The king said to me — with the queen seated beside Him — "How long will your journey take, and when will you return?" It pleased the king to send me, and I set him a time.

I said to the king again, "If it pleases the king, may there be granted royal letters to me for the governors of the province west-bank of the Euphrates river, so that they will permit me to travel through their lands until I reach the province of Judah.

And a royal letter to Asaph, keeper of the royal garden of the king, that he may give me timber for making beams of the gates of the citadel of the temple, for the city wall, and for the house I will enter to occupy." The king granted these things to me, because the gracious hand of my God was with me.
I then went to the governors of the province west-bank of the Euphrates river and gave them the royal letters of the king. The king also sent army officers and cavalry-horsemen with me.
When Sanballat the Horonite, and Tobiah the servant the Ammonite, heard this news, they became greatly displeased that someone had come to seek the welfare of the people of Israel.
I came to Jerusalem, and stayed there three days.
I rose up at night, with only a few men, and I did not tell anyone what my God had laid on my heart to do for Jerusalem. There was no other animal with me except the animal I was riding.

At night I went out through the Valley Gate, past the Dragon Spring, and on to the Rubbish Gate. I inspected the walls of Jerusalem which had been broken down, and the gates of the city which had been burned with fire.
Then I went on to the Fountain Gate and the King's Pool, but there was no space for the animal I was riding to pass through.
So at night I went up along the brook, and inspected the wall, then turned back and entered through the Valley Gate, and returned home.
The officials did not know where I had gone or what I was doing, because I had not yet told the Jewish leaders, the priests, the nobles, the officials, or the ones who would do the work.

Then I said to them, "You see the distress in which we have fallen — Jerusalem lies in ruins, and the gates of the city have been burned with fire. Come, let us rebuild the wall of Jerusalem, so that we will no longer be a disgrace."

I told them of the gracious hand of my God which was with me, and the words of the king which He had spoken to me. They replied, "Let us rise up and build," and they set their hands to this good work with their strength.
When Sanballat the Horonite, Tobiah the Ammonite servant, and Geshem the Arabian heard this, they mocked us and despised us, saying, "What is this you are doing? Are you rebelling against the king?"
I answered them, "The God of heaven Himself is the One who will grant us success. As for us, His servants, we will rise up and build. But you have no portion, no right, and no memorial in Jerusalem."
